author: @marjon1703
text: Love the fact that you interject 'how to' videos too. They help me to detail what's actually going on in these magic gearboxes :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Glad you like them!

author: @yodaco
text: Its sad to say that nothing is perfect but severe vibrations at any decent speed are the issue with these unless you are able to balance them out which limits your ratio options severely for sure. Still as with all such things it has its uses.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to balance it just put two gears in the ring - but to be honest - I suspect you know that already

replies.author: @yodaco
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering oh yes sir, I've played harmonic drive whack-a-mole for a few rounds.

replies.author: @yodaco
replies.text: Conclusion being only few ratios will survive being rattled to death no matter how robust (pretty robust) you try to build them. It's just as nature intended it I believe. But establishing the rules for this is beyond my budget unfortunately.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @yodaco I am sorry mate - i hate to disagree - but that just doesn't sound right - the basic principle here is the cycloid drive and the responses of that are well established - plenty of research on multiple disk configurations for exactly what you are saying

replies.author: @yodaco
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering well look from my use cases and testing they are too fussy to be practical and do the opposite of what they should do alot more than they should. that has to do with a lot of variable you cannot easily deal with in a small team with low resources. in short only certain and very specfic configurations work well this is a harmonic thing or something like that you cannot easily measure. now we are talking about high speed high torque high load high everything situations here and not does it work on the bench but honestly its just very very difficult to use if it werent we would see them more. they rip them selves to bits to easily. they are great and worth learning about. my job is finding problems more than anything.

author: @AKA-f7p
text:
I saw this before.
In the wankel rotary engine Because this thing is shaft and in reverse direction.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - no you didn't mate - you saw an eccentric - they are quite common - steam engines had them

replies.author: @AKA-f7p
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkeringi think you didn't get it.
This drive's stator is rotar in the wankel engine.
Similarly rotary is stator in the wankel engine.
The shaft is output in the wankel engine.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @AKA-f7p cheers mate

replies.author: @AKA-f7p
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkeringok then! Cheers.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: I assume that the inside gear doesn't have to be the only one that travels eccentrically. It should be possible to make the outer gear travel in an centric motion instead. The reason I ask is that I have something I want to work with where by the outside would have to travel in the eccentric manor.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice thinking mate - let me know how you get on

author: @EastBayFlipper
text:
It would be interesting to see how a herringbone gear would impact the design 🤔 
At first glance, I would imagine a herringbone pattern would use more surface and transmit more torque but would increase friction. 🤔 but how much?
This would be a very cool design for a manually operated winch in steel, of course. ❤
Thanks for the imagination seeds😁👍🍻

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: let me know if you do a herringbone version mate - i'd love to see it

author: @ElizabethGreene
text: I wonder how much this will vibrate at high speed.  In cycloidal drives you can balance this with a second rotor 180° out of phase, but I don't see how that could work for this application.  Perhaps that might work if the external gears were fixed and the housing rotated?  Off to experiment!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That is exactly what you would do - just offset another eccentric at 180 degrees and add a second disk - I meant to mention this in the vid but forgot
